retrieval_events.query: Sydney: Honestly, it brings up a lot of mixed emotions. There’s definitely a sense of frustration, like I’m angry at the expectations that were set for me, but at the same time, I feel this sadness because I know it comes from a place of wanting to protect and support each other. It’s like I can see both sides, but it doesn’t make it any easier to deal with. I often feel this weight of disappointment in myself for not being able to live up to those standards, and it’s exhausting. I guess there’s also a bit of guilt mixed in, like I should be able to handle things better or be more resilient. It’s tough because I want to break free from that cycle, but I feel like I’m constantly battling against these ingrained beliefs about what it means to be strong or successful. It’s just a lot to carry, and sometimes I wonder if I’ll ever find a way to navigate it without feeling so overwhelmed.

retrieval_events.query: Sydney: You know, I think those beliefs really shape how I approach just about everything. It’s like I have this constant internal dialogue that tells me I need to be perfect or at least close to it. So, when challenges come up, I often feel this overwhelming pressure to handle them on my own, like asking for help would somehow be a sign of weakness. I find myself pushing through, even when I’m feeling overwhelmed, because I don’t want to let anyone down or admit that I’m struggling. It’s exhausting, honestly. I think it makes me more rigid in my thinking, too. Instead of being open to different solutions or ways of handling things, I get stuck on this idea that I have to do it a certain way, which just adds to my anxiety. I guess it’s like I’m trying to uphold this image of strength, but in reality, it just leaves me feeling more isolated and stressed. I wish I could find a way to be more flexible and compassionate with myself, but it feels like such a tall order sometimes.

It’s like giving myself permission to be human, you know? I imagine it would look like taking a step back and recognizing that it’s okay to not have everything figured out all the time. Maybe it would involve reaching out for support instead of trying to handle everything alone, even if that feels uncomfortable. I guess it would also mean being kinder in my self-talk, like replacing those harsh judgments with more understanding thoughts. It’s just hard to picture that sometimes because I’ve been so conditioned to push through and keep up that façade of strength. I think if I could find a way to embrace that vulnerability, it might actually help me feel more connected to others and less overwhelmed. But, of course, that’s easier said than done, right?

It feels a bit scary to think about, but I guess it could help me practice being more honest about my struggles. I could also try to catch myself when those harsh judgments pop up and consciously replace them with something gentler. It’s definitely a work in progress, but I think starting with those little moments of honesty could be a way to ease into it. It might feel a bit vulnerable to ask for that support, but I guess that’s part of the process, right? And maybe I could also give myself some grace when I slip up, reminding myself that it’s okay to not get it perfect right away. I just really want to make sure I don’t lose sight of these intentions when life gets busy or stressful. It’s like I need to create a little safety net for myself to fall back on.

I worry about stumbling over my words or not expressing myself clearly, which just adds to the anxiety. I guess there’s also that fear of how my friend might react—what if they don’t respond the way I hope? That uncertainty can be really paralyzing. I mean, I want to be honest and share what I’m feeling, but there’s this voice in my head that keeps telling me to hold back, to protect myself from potential judgment or misunderstanding. It’s frustrating because I know that opening up could lead to something positive, but that fear of vulnerability can be really strong.

topic_probe_events.client_reply: Sydney: I mean, yeah, I guess that makes sense. It’s just, I feel like I’m always thinking about my stomach and what I can do to fix it. It’s like it takes over my mind, you know? I try to distract myself or focus on other things, but then I end up back at square one, worrying about every little thing I eat or how I feel. I don’t want to be consumed by this, but sometimes it feels like it’s all I can think about. It’s exhausting, really. I just wish I could find a way to let it go, but it’s like it keeps pulling me back in.

topic_probe_events.prompt_context:
- Institutional fear (Law)

Explicitly discuss this topic in normal, client-friendly language. Ask whether it fits the client, while leaving room for them to say it does not.
topic_probe_events.client_reply: Sydney: Yeah, I think that makes sense. It’s like, if I let go of the worry, what if something bad happens and I’m not paying attention? I mean, I know it sounds a bit irrational, but it’s hard to shake that feeling. It’s almost like I feel responsible for keeping track of everything, like if I don’t worry, I’m somehow neglecting my health. I guess there’s this fear that if I stop being so vigilant, I might miss something important. It’s a bit of a double-edged sword, really. I want to feel better, but then I’m scared of what that might mean if I stop worrying. It’s just a lot to juggle, you know?

topic_probe_events.prompt_context:
- Sleep disturbance (Health)

Explicitly discuss this topic in normal, client-friendly language. Ask whether it fits the client, while leaving room for them to say it does not.
topic_probe_events.client_reply: Sydney: Yeah, I mean, sleep has definitely been an issue too. I find myself lying awake, just replaying everything in my mind—what I ate that day, how I felt, what I should have done differently. It’s like my brain just won’t shut off. I know I need rest, but the worry keeps me up, and then I feel even more drained the next day. It’s a vicious cycle, really. I want to be able to just relax and not think about all of this, but it feels like it’s always there, lurking in the background. I guess I just wish I could find a way to quiet those thoughts, you know?

topic_probe_events.prompt_context:
- Pain or fatigue (Health)

Explicitly discuss this topic in normal, client-friendly language. Ask whether it fits the client, while leaving room for them to say it does not.
topic_probe_events.client_reply: Sydney: Yeah, I think that does fit. I mean, when I’m feeling anxious, it definitely takes a toll on my body. I notice I get more tense, and sometimes I have this tightness in my stomach that just adds to everything else. It’s like my body is reacting to all the stress, and then that makes me worry more about my health, which just keeps the cycle going. I can feel fatigued too, especially when I haven’t slept well. It’s like I’m fighting against my own body, trying to manage the anxiety while also dealing with how it physically affects me. I just wish I could find a way to break that connection, you know? It feels like I’m stuck in this loop where everything feeds into each other, and it’s hard to find a way out.

- Overcontrol pattern (Coping)

topic_probe_events.client_reply: Sydney: Yeah, that definitely resonates. I think there’s this part of me that feels like I need to have everything figured out before I even start. It’s like I want to control the outcome, and if I can’t guarantee that it’ll work, I hesitate to even try. I mean, I know that’s not how it should be, but it’s hard to shake that feeling. I guess I just want to feel like I’m making progress, but the fear of not doing it right or not seeing results just holds me back. It’s frustrating because I know I need to do something different, but then I get stuck in this cycle of overthinking it all. It’s like I’m my own worst enemy sometimes, you know?

topic_probe_events.client_reply: Sydney: Yeah, that definitely resonates. I mean, it’s like my mind just keeps circling back to those same worries, and it feels like I can’t break free from that pattern. I get caught up in replaying things, whether it’s about my health, my choices, or even past experiences. It’s almost like I’m stuck in a mental hamster wheel, and no matter how hard I try, I can’t seem to jump off. I know it’s not helping me, but it’s like I’m drawn to those thoughts, almost like they’re familiar or something. It’s frustrating because I want to move forward, but I feel like I’m constantly looking back instead. I guess I just wish I could find a way to shift my focus and not get so trapped in those loops.

Explicitly discuss this topic in normal, client-friendly language. Ask whether it fits the client, while leaving room for them to say it does not.
topic_probe_events.client_reply: Sydney: You know, I think it’s a bit of both, honestly. There’s definitely some pressure from my family, especially around expectations and how to handle stress. I mean, growing up, it felt like there was this unspoken rule that you had to keep everything together and not show any weakness. So, I guess I internalized that and now I feel like I have to manage everything on my own, like I can’t let anyone see me struggle. But then there’s also this internal voice that just amplifies those feelings, telling me I should be able to handle it all. It’s like I’m caught between wanting to meet those external expectations and my own unrealistic standards. I wish I could just let go of that pressure, but it feels so deeply rooted. It’s frustrating because I know it’s not helping me, but it’s hard to break free from that mindset.
